steps.text: Using the y-axis scale, the bars for 2011 and 2014 rise to around the 37 mark, clearly above 22, and 2012 reaches a bit under 30 (around 28), also above 22.
The 2013 bar is much shorter, sitting around the 15 level, which is below 22. So, 2011, 2012, and 2014 are over 22—making three years.

Using the legend colors to compare the stacked segments for South Korea versus France within each year on the x‑axis, the France segment is clearly taller only in 2014. In 2015 the two segments look about even (South Korea is essentially level with France), so 2015 already counts toward South Korea.
From 2015 onward the South Korea segment is at least as tall as France, and from 2016 it is clearly taller, staying that way through 2017 and 2018.

In Jan–Oct 2019, the South Korea slice is again higher than France (South Korea sits a bit above the 0.25 level while France is below 0.20), confirming South Korea is larger in 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, and Jan–Oct 2019.
Scanning left to right along the x-axis (1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2019), the line keeps climbing and reaches its highest point at the far-right 2019 mark, just above 60.
Because 2019 sits at the top of the line (about 60.6 million, higher than every earlier year), 2019 is the year with the greatest increase.

Scanning along the x‑axis seasons, the bars at or near the $60 level are the first seven seasons: 2006/07, 2007/08, 2008/09, 2009/10, 2010/11, 2011/12, and 2012/13—each of these is counted toward the below-$60 group, while 2013/14 and later clearly rise above it.
Reading those seven bar heights (about 52.5, 57, 54.5, 53.5, 51.5, 51.5, 63.1) and averaging them gives roughly $54.79.
Scanning the points from 2000 to 2008 along the x-axis,
the smallest point is in 2001, sitting just under the 0.09 level (about 0.086).
The largest in that span is around 2003–2004 near 0.11; the second largest is the point in 2002, which sits a bit above 0.10 (about 0.105).

Subtracting the visually read values gives roughly 0.105 − 0.086 ≈ 0.019, but because the 2002 point is clearly above 0.10 and the 2001 point is clearly below 0.09, the difference reads closer to about 0.02, and using the plotted positions it’s about 0.023.
Scanning the x‑axis from 2010 through 2015,
Germany’s line reaches its highest point around 2010, sitting right at about the 50% level on the y‑axis.
Over the same 2010–2015 span, the U.S. line dips to its lowest point at 2014, where the marker sits just under the 20% tick (about 19%).
Subtracting these visually read values (about 50% − 19%) gives a difference of roughly 31 points.
Identify the maximum number of fires in the data (380).
Then take the average level of fires across the years, which is 238.75.
Finally, subtract the average from the maximum: 380 − 238.75 = 141.25.
Look at the 2019 “Share of respondents” values for each diet type and count those that reach 0.03 or more (0.03 and above).

The diet types at 0.03 or above in 2019 are: Clean eating (0.10), Intermittent fasting (0.09), Gluten-free diet (0.06), Low-carb diet (0.06), Ketogenic or high-fat diet (0.06), Plant-based diet (0.05), Weight-loss plan (0.05), Mediterranean diet (0.05), Vegetarian or vegan diet (0.03), Paleo Diet (0.03), Flexitarian (0.03), and DASH diet (0.03). 
That makes 12 diet types.
Identify Scotland’s largest SharePercentage by scanning its values; the maximum is 28 (Strongly against). Then identify England & Wales’ smallest SharePercentage; the minimum is 2 (Strongly against).
Subtract the smallest England & Wales value from the largest Scotland value to get 28 − 2 = 26.
Identify the three highest per-square-foot costs in the data: 732 (Sydney), 646 (Melbourne), and 422 (Brisbane Mall).
Add these three values and divide by 3 to get their average, which equals 600.

Use the fatalities values for all years (2000–2020). 
Compute the average by summing all yearly fatalities and dividing by the number of years. 
Compute the median by sorting the values and taking the middle one (since there are 21 years, it’s the 11th value).

The data has several higher values in the early 2000s (around 200–221) and many lower values later (down to 85), which pulls the median down more than the mean. 
After calculating both, the mean is about 166.6 while the median is 173, so the median is actually higher than the mean.

For each year, compare “Russia favorability in U.S.” against “U.S. favorability in Russia.” 
Russia’s value is higher in 2007 (44 vs 41) and 2015 (22 vs 15). 
In all other listed years, the U.S. value is higher, so Russia is higher in 2 years total.

Take the last five years in the data (2015–2020). 
For each of these years, compute the difference as (Female population − Male population): 2015: 670.48−704.14, 2016: 674.56−708.15, 2017: 678.71−711.37, 2018: 681.87−713.51, 2019: 684.78−715.27, 2020: 688.44−723.34.
Then add these yearly differences together to get a total of -196.94 million inhabitants.
For each city, compare the population value in 2025 to the value in 2010 and compute the increase.

Tokyo is by far the largest city on the chart, towering over every other bar in both years. Since Tokyo dominates the chart, it clearly has the biggest jump in population. Therefore, Tokyo has the biggest jump.
and add them together to get the total sum.
Take the “Very interested” shares for the non-Black race/ethnicity groups (White = 0.20, Hispanic = 0.16, Other = 0.21).

Compute their average: (0.20 + 0.16 + 0.21) / 3 = 0.19). 
Convert 0.19 to a percentage, which is 19%.

Review each year’s FIFA World Ranking position and count those whose value is above 20 and at most 30. The qualifying years are 2003 (27), 2004 (27), 2009 (29), 2012 (27), 2013 (27), 2014 (26), 2015 (28), 2016 (25), 2017 (22), and 2018 (23). That makes 10 years in total.
Treat “Europe region” as the European categories in the chart: Germany, Great Britain, Spain, and Rest of Europe. Consider only years before 2015 (2011–2014).
Sum the employees across those four regions for each of the years 2011, 2012, 2013, and 2014, then add those yearly totals together to get 230,769.
First, compute the overall average Consumer Price Index using all the months shown (May ’20 through May ’21).
Then compute the average CPI within each year (average of all 2020 months vs. average of all 2021 months).
Compare how far each year’s average is from the overall average (the absolute deviation).
The 2021 average is farther above the overall average than the 2020 average is below it, so 2021 has the biggest deviation from the total average.

Count the Boys-year values that are greater than 3670: 2010 (3672), 2011 (3677), and 2014 (3675) → 3 values. 
Then count the Girls-year values that are greater than 3550: 2010 (3580), 2011 (3553), 2014 (3554), 2017 (3555), 2020 (3555) → 5 values. 
Add the two counts: 3 + 5 = 8.

For each year, compare the “Price” value to the “Convenience” value and compute the gap (Price − Convenience). 
The gaps are: 2012 = 20, 2013 = 15, 2014 = 22, 2015 = 16, 2016 = 19.
The smallest gap is 15, which occurs in 2013.

The North America bars sit around the 9–11 range on the y-axis in most years (e.g., near 9 in 2009/2011 and a bit above 10 in 2012), while the Latin America bars cluster just above 1 (roughly 1.0–1.3) across all years.
Averaging across the five year panels gives North America about 9.58 and Latin America about 1.16, so the gap between their averages is about 9.58 − 1.16 ≈ 8.42 billion.
Compare the average lease length in 2003 versus mid-2013 for each sector and compute the decline (2003 value minus mid-2013 value).
Large companies drop from 9.5 years to 5.2 years, a decline of 4.3 years, which is larger than the declines in Retail (3.5), All (2.3), SMEs (0.9), Offices (0.8), and Industrial (1.5).
Look at the agriculture-coloured band across the years and compare its height.
The agriculture band is tallest in 2017, so agriculture is highest in 2017.
For each method, compare the “EVERY DAY” share to the “LESS OFTEN” share and take the absolute difference.
Text messaging has 0.55 for every day and 0.13 for less often, giving a difference of 0.42, which is larger than the differences for all other methods.
